repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

origination.label: creator
origination.corpname: Stephan Loewentheil History of Photography Collection

did.abstract: Collection of photographs on various subjects demonstrating the technological evolution of photography, with emphasis on the period of 1822 to 1885. Includes 19th- and 20th-century photographs organized in several major groups.

scopecontent.head: Scope and content
scopecontent.p: Collection of photographs on various subjects demonstrating the technological evolution of photography, with emphasis on the period of 1822 to 1885. Includes 19th- and 20th-century photographs organized in several major groups: Daguerre ephemera and early photographic processes, African American collection, Mathew Brady and the Civil War, Lincoln, Native Americans and the West, hand-colored photography, and cartes de visite. The collection contains daguerreotypes, salted paper photographs, ambrotypes, tintypes, albumen prints, cartes de visite, cabinet cards, stereographs, and photograph albums. Also included are lithographic prints (some hand colored), a graphite on paper portrait, wood engraving, medals, and stamp, as well as early photograph viewers such as a "hemisferium praxinoscope" and a "photographicon."

defitem.label: Contact Information:
defitem.item: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections 2B Carl A. Kroch Library Cornell University Ithaca, NY 14853 (607) 255-3530 Fax: (607) 255-9524rareref@cornell.eduhttp://rmc.library.cornell.edu

odd.head: NOTES
odd.p: Associated book collection cataloged separately.See the "Separated Material" section below for a list of the CDV albums which have also been cataloged individually.

prefercite.head: Cite As:
prefercite.p: Stephan Loewentheil Photograph Collection, #8043. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: The collection includes some historical images and description that reveal racist, disturbing, or otherwise negative representations or stereotypes of the people depicted. These images and descriptions have been included as part of the historical record and should not be interpreted to mean that Cornell University or its staff endorse or approve of the negative representations or stereotypes presented.

relatedmaterial.head: RELATED MATERIALS
relatedmaterial.p: An on-line exhibit of "Dawn's Early Light" showcasing many items from this collection.
An on-line exhibit of "Lincoln's Unfinished Work" showcasing many of the Lincoln related items from this collection.

This modern praxinoscope is decorated on the top and outside with a circus theme and the designation "hemisferium." The pictures on the strips to be viewed include dancing figures, tumblers, and a toy soldier on a rocking horse.
did.abstract: Invented by Charles-Émile Reynaud in 1877, the praxinoscope was an animation device, the successor to the zoetrope. It used a strip of pictures placed around the inner surface of a spinning cylinder. An inner circle of mirrors was placed so that the reflections of the pictures appeared more or less stationary in position as the wheel turned. The viewer looking in the mirrors would thus see a rapid succession of images, producing the illusion of motion.

did.unittitle: Harper's Weekly and The Daily Graphic articles featuring illustrations of horses

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two illustrations from Harper's Weekly and one from The Daily Graphic depicting the way horses were traditionally drawn before inventions in photography allowed photographers to capture a horse in motion to illustrate the movement.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Alphabetical by name and by subject

did.unittitle: Brady, Lincoln, and the Civil War

did.abstract: Photograph viewer in the form of a wooden box covered in velvet, containing two rollers, with a viewing window on the top. By turning one of two knobs on the front of the device, the viewer can move forward or back through a roll of photographs on the two rollers. The roll contains many reproductions of engravings or lithographs in addition to (unidentified) portrait photographs. The images are in carte de visite format, but inserted into a continuous roll of paper photo frames. Images include a photo of a Union army officer from the Civil War.

did.unittitle: Guide to the Loewentheil family collection of cartes-de-visite

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The contents of this binder were created by the Loewentheil family in creating the collection. Sitter names and descriptions for many of the CDVs, both those individual and those bound in albums (listed under separated materials), are included. Locations may differ from current object locations within the collection.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains a carte-de-visit of a Civil War era colorist at work. Most portraits are unidentified. Identified sitters include Sarah E. Marcy, Catherine Wentz Atwood, Mary Elizabeth Eastwood, Mrs. Frances Browning Marey, Sallie Fisher, Ann Mitchell, Mrs. Christian B. Mayer, Mrs. James P. Cook, Catherine B. Heron, Elizabeth Smiley Helligose, Mrs. George D'Almaine, Maria Parker Silkman, and Cecilia Grant. Photographers include Broadbent and Co., George A. Lenzf, G.M. Elton, Nathan H. DeVars, Walter Dinmore and Co., Erekson and Bodurtha, R. Newell, W.E. Christie, M. Ellsmoore, W.E. Entrekin, and H. Manger and Co.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains portraits of children and infants, almost all unidentified. Several portraits are of children holding pet dogs. Photographers include J. Carroll McCaffrey, Jones and Brother, A.M. Burrough, D.A. Clifford, J.M. Horning, Erekson and Bodurtha, and L. Waite.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Identified sitters include John Woodruff, Percival Roberts Sr., John Richards, Jeremiah Chapman (Box III-25) and Rev. J.G. Barton. All others unidentified. Photographers and artists include Charles Fredericks, J.A. Keenan, Irving M. Smith, H.R. Couch, R.A. Lewis, William S. Pendleton, Charles Cohill, Broadbent and Co., Elmira Portrait Co., J. Kolbeck, R.N. Keeley, L. Horning, J.H. Carpenter, Fred G. Milander, Terkelson and Henry Studios, Erekson and Bodurtha, and James Cremer.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes portraits of soldiers, officers, and a hand-colored photograph of Abraham Lincoln, based on an original photograph by Anthony Berger. Other identified sitters include Major Hawksworth, Enoch W. Greene, Isaac DeYoung, Major Ivan Hicks, and L.A. Leighton. Most portraits are unidentified. Photographers include J.R. Hall, L. Horning, B.F. Reimer, and J. Kolbeck.

did.unittitle: Historian George Sullivan's Mathew Brady Research Archive

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Historian George Sullivan, author of Mathew Brady: His Life and Photographs (1994) and In the Wake of Battle: The Civil War Images of Mathew Brady (2004) was one of the foremost authorities on the life and work of Mathew Brady. This collection, organized in three-ring binders and file folders, comprises correspondence, notes, web printouts, photocopies, photographic prints, and tear sheets, represents Sullivan's lifetime reference archive.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Charles A. Leale was the first physician to attend President Abraham Lincoln after Lincoln was shot at Ford's Theatre on April 14, 1865. This photograph shows the physician and his family at their home. Included are Dr. Leale, his wife Rebecca Medwin Copcutt Leale, and their children Annie, Lilian, Medwin, Marion, and Loyal.
